coredata.dc:title: Bioinspired quantum dots for cancer therapy: A mini-review
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Arun
Singh, Kshitij RB
Ghate, Manjunath D.
Lalhlenmawia, H.
Kumar, Deepak
Singh, Jay
coredata.dc:description: The global burden of cancer increases with the passing year and is becoming the primary cause of death globally. Chemotherapy plays an important role but produces side effects, natural compounds seem to be an option with fewer side effects, but their bioavailability remains a concern. Bioinspired Quantum dots (QDs) from plant sources show promising drug delivery and anti-cancer potential with low toxicity. QDs are well known for their optical activity and find their use in various applications and can be used explicitly for bio-imaging and drug delivery in cancer therapy. Further, bioinspired QDs as an anti-cancer drug delivery system is less explored. Thus, this featured article aims to conclude the recent advances in the use of bioinspired QDs for drug delivery in cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioinspired Quantum dots
Carbon Quantum dots
Anti-cancer
Natural product
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bioinspired QDs and their application in drug delivery
Challenges of bioinspired QDs in drug delivery:

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different nanoformulations used in drug delivery systems.
Structure of some natural product based QD formulations.

A schematic illustration of the release of DOX by folic acid synthesized MSN-CDs to the targeted cancer cell (reproduced with permission from Zhao et al. (2019) [30]; distributed under attribution CC BY 4.0).

Systematic illustration of the preparation of carbon dots and DOX-complexed CDs-Hep/DOX and the release of drug as a response of pH (reproduced from Zhang et al. (2017) [31] with permission from the Royal Society of Chemistry).
List of different natural products used in combination with QDs.

row.entry: QD and CQDs as a carrier for natural product

Drug loaded nanocapsules combined with lactoferrin tagged QDs

head.ce:title: Bioinspired quantum dots for cancer therapy: A mini-review

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Shoolini University, Solan 173229, Himachal Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Regional Institute of Paramedical and Nursing Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Regional Institute of Paramedical and Nursing Sciences, Aizawl 796017, Mizoram, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The global burden of cancer increases with the passing year and is becoming the primary cause of death globally. Chemotherapy plays an important role but produces side effects, natural compounds seem to be an option with fewer side effects, but their bioavailability remains a concern. Bioinspired Quantum dots (QDs) from plant sources show promising drug delivery and anti-cancer potential with low toxicity. QDs are well known for their optical activity and find their use in various applications and can be used explicitly for bio-imaging and drug delivery in cancer therapy. Further, bioinspired QDs as an anti-cancer drug delivery system is less explored. Thus, this featured article aims to conclude the recent advances in the use of bioinspired QDs for drug delivery in cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a leading cause of death globally; as of 2020, nearly 19.3 million cancer cases were seen worldwide, out of which 10.0 million died, and lung cancer was found to be the leading cause of death [1]. Cancer cases have been increasing continuously, with just 10 million cases in the year 2000 increased to 14.1 million in 2012, and in the year 2020, it further increased to 19.3 million cases [1–4]. There are different treatment methods for cancer, but chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery are still the most common [5]. Even after improved efficacy, side effects associated with chemotherapy remain a major concern [6]. Although, plant-based compounds are considered safer, less toxic, and eco-friendly than conventional treatment. Many phytoconstituents are known to have anti-cancer properties viz artemisinins, vincristine, vinblastine, paclitaxel, docetaxel [7–9]. Phytoconstituents have good activity, but their decreased bioavailability remains a challenge. Therefore, another form of drug delivery system is needed [10].

Moreover, nanotechnology uses different nanoparticles for drug delivery, such as dendrimers, liposomes, nanopores, nanoemulsions, etc., as shown in Fig. 1 . Nanoparticles provide a promising drug delivery system but determining the fate of the drug in-vivo is not possible [11]. Quantum dots (QDs) are a semiconducting cluster of atoms prepared artificially and have interesting optical properties with a typical size range of 2–20 nm [12]. QDs were first created in the 1980s in Texas Instruments Incorporated [13]; and have been used in various applications since then, such as cancer diagnosis and therapy [12]. They can be used to target and track specific cancer, as studies show that folates decorated nano-formulation specifically target folate receptor-positive where QDs helped to determine in-vivo drug release and drug targeting [14–17]. Moreover, there is much literature data available, highlighting various uses of nanomaterials and quantum dots in the biomedical domain [18–21]; but, this featured article focuses on the recent advances in the use of a combination of plant compounds and quantum dots in cancer therapy. It will help gain the attention of various researchers and healthcare practitioners.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioinspired QDs and their application in drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: QDs in drug delivery of natural products provide beneficiary advantages. Belletti et al., 2017 reported curcumin-loaded PLGA nanoparticles with QDs and evaluated cytotoxic activity on BCBL-1 and HBL-6 cell lines. The loading process increased the bioavailability and hence the activity of curcumin [22]. Paclitaxel and (CdTe/CdS/ZnS) QDs co-loaded NLC's (nanostructure lipid carriers) prepared by emulsion evaporation and low temperature-solidification methods were subjected to in-vitro and in-vivo studies. The drug release of co-loaded NLCs included fast initial release during the first 8 h followed by a slow, prolonged release. Due to slow-release, the IC50 value for co-loaded NLC was reported at 1.05 ± 0.58 μM; that was much higher than the IC50 value of Taxol® (0.07 ± 0.04 μM). But, a higher tumor inhibition was seen in the case of the prepared formulation, about 77.85%, which is 26.9% more than for Taxol® [23]. Cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) decorated with Fe3O4-Ag2O quantum dots attached Methotrexate and Etoposide showed in-vitro drug release and anti-cancer activity. Drug release with a constant speed was seen for Etoposide reaching equilibrium with 78.94% after 24 h. A relatively slow release was seen for methotrexate, reaching equilibrium with 63.84% after 30 h. Cytotoxicity of the sample was dose-dependent decreasing cell viability to about 32% after a dose of 1 gm/ml for methotrexate; however, the study suggests that Fe3O4-Ag2O QDs/Cellulose fibers nano-composites are safe to use as they produced no cytotoxic effects by themselves [24]. AbdElhamid et al. prepared lactoferrin-tagged cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots-based nanocapsules loaded with honokiol and celecoxib (LF–QDs–CS–NCs) via layer by layer assembly technique. Enhanced internalization, tumor growth inhibition, and slow drug release were observed in LF–QDs–CS–NCs than LF-CS-NCs, CS-NCs, and free drug combinations. Besides that, QDs on/off state was seen depending on whether it has attached to lactoferrin or not. These activities relate to caspase-3, tumor angiogenic marker VEGF-1 inhibition, apoptotic enzyme activation, and p-AKT level reduction [25]. Celastrol or CSL (a traditional Chinese medicine) conjugated Cys-CdTe QDs were prepared and then subjected to in-vitro evaluations on sensitive K562 and resistant K562/ A02 cells. A pH-sensitive drug release was seen in the synthesized QDs, in specific pH-dependent targeting of the drug. The CSL conjugated Cys-CdTe QDs produced more toxicity than the CSL alone due to increased drug internalization. An increase in apoptosis rate was seen in the case of QD-treated cells [26].

Although most quantum dots are considered cytotoxic due to the presence of heavy metals like cadmium, carbon-based quantum dots have shown low toxicity, making them more biocompatible [27,28]. C-dots or Carbon dots typically are small nanoparticles with an almost spherical shape. They have either a crystalline or an amorphous core with sp2 hybridized carbon sheets fused by sp3 hybridized carbon sheets containing oxygen. Moreover, with the onset of green chemistry, the production of green-based carbon dots has deliberately increased as it increases biocompatibility, reduces side-effects of harmful chemicals, and is more cheap and convenient. The different structures of naturally produced carbon quantum dots are illustrated in Fig. 2 . Whereas in Table 1 , natural products used in combination with carbon quantum dots are listed. It was found that the carbon quantum dots exhibit a higher inhibition effect on various cancer cell lines [27]. Sawant et al. synthesized curcumin-loaded ginger herb-based carbon dot coated TiO2 nanoparticles. Curcumin was chosen due to its hydrophobicity, which reduces its bioavailability, and QDs were synthesized by the green hydrothermal method. Curcumin-loaded carbon dot coated TiO2 increased the compound's anti-cancer and anti-psoriatic activity by destroying its hydrophobicity, increasing bioavailability [29].

Similarly, it was found that the fluorescent mesoporous-silica carbon dots nanohybrid (MSN-CDs-NH) exhibit great potential in inhibiting cancer cells as they can easily target the folate receptors-overexpressing cancer cells like HeLa cells. The MSN-CDs were highly biocompatible as CDs were prepared in-situ from the folic acid and were further bonded on the façade of amino-modified MSN-NH with the help of microwave-assisted solvothermal reaction, as in Fig. 3 . Moreover, MSN-CDs' excellent fluorescence properties helped them maintain the individual characteristics, such as high biocompatibility, mesoporous structure, high surface to volume ratio, etc. It also exhibited stable and strong yellow emission, which resulted in developing utility in the diagnosis of fluorescence imaging-guided drug delivery by real-time fluorescence tracking and proves that the MSN-CD-NH can be efficiently used for both diagnostic and therapeutic applications, resulting in decreasing the side-effects of toxic chemotherapeutic drugs, while simultaneously increasing the delivery of drugs to the targeted sites [30].

An interesting pH-based study showed that water-soluble carbon dots conjugated with anti-coagulant heparin (Hep) and anti-cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) prevented clotting of blood but also efficiently delivered the DOX at the targeted cancer cells, as shown in Fig. 4 . The high-drug loading capacity of CD for DOX and Hep was 32.2% and 28.45%, respectively. Further, it was reported that the DOX loaded with Hep-CD was more effective in preventing the spread of cancer cells, and the Hep helped increase blood compatibility. Due to the presence of electrostatic interactions, the DOX and Hep were easily delivered in highly acidic conditions. Moreover, the experiment results also indicated that CDs can easily reduce the side-effects of DOX and Hep and can be further used for the image-guided drug-delivery for image-guided cancer therapy [31]. Shivaji et al., 2018 synthesized Camellia sinensis CdS-QDs with a 2–5 nm particle size. These NPs showed a cytotoxic effect on A549 cell lines (lung cancer) comparable to that of cisplatin and acted by arresting cell growth in the S phase of the cell cycle [32].

Preparation of CQDs from the plant sources is a recent approach where phytoconstituents are used. Ginsenoside Re (genus Panax) based carbon dots were synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal process in the year 2018 by Yao et al. The synthesized carbon dot proved to be active against cancer cell lines (MCF-7, HepG-2, and A375) while sparing the healthy cells (293 T). There was a relationship between cancer cell proliferation and apoptosis induction to caspase mediation and increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels [33]. Black pepper, turmeric, red chili, and cinnamon carbon dots (aqueous fluorescent) were synthesized using a one-pot green synthesis method and subjected to in-vitro studies. Cytotoxicity studies on human kidney cells (HK-2) and human glioblastoma cells (LN- 229) revealed a higher cytotoxic effect on LN-229 cells. Bioimaging suggested higher uptake of C-dots in the cancerous cell, while a low uptake was seen in non-cancerous cells [34]. Further, Boobalan et al. synthesized fluorescent C-dots (blue/green) from oyster mushrooms by hydrothermal synthesis and evaluated anti-cancer activity on breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB-231) and normal embryonic kidney cells (HEK-293). The study suggested that the C-dots inhibited the growth of cancerous cells more effectively than the non-cancerous cells with an IC50 value of 3.34 μg/ml for MDA-MB-231 and cell viability decreasing to just 70% at 25 μg/ml for HEK-293 cells, making the C-dots more selective. Alongside these, C-dots also acted as antibacterial, a Pb + ion, and intercalative DNA binding compound sensors. [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges of bioinspired QDs in drug delivery:
ce:section.ce:para: QDs are an excellent drug delivery medium with the advantage of bioimaging because of their fluorescence properties, but their specific targeting and heavy metal toxicity are a challenge. Specific targeting can be achieved for some cancer like FR positive by incorporating the receptor-ligand on the nanoparticles. Plant-based CQDs provide an alternative to avoid toxicity, but their fate in the biological system can be unprecedented. Moreover, the lack of in vivo studies restricts broad insight into their effects inside a living system.

QDs are mostly used for imaging purposes combined with different nanoparticles to observe drug targeting and release in anti-cancer studies. In some cases, QDs helped enhance the drug activity, but such studies with anti-cancer phytochemicals are not often seen. QDs produce cytotoxic effects. Although coating the core helps eliminate the toxic effects of QDs, heavy metals always remain a health concern. Carbon dots prepared from natural sources reduce such health concerns related to the use of these quasi-spherical particles. Carbon dots possess optical properties and can produce improved anti-cancer effects by themselves if prepared from a natural product exhibiting some amount of anti-cancer activity. Such carbon dots also showed specificity toward cancer cells. The challenges associated with CQDs in drug delivery systems can be overcome by in-depth in vivo studies, and specific targeting can be achieved by attaching specific receptor ligands to the CQDs.

The onset of nanotechnology has increased the therapeutic standards and taken the technology to new levels. The idea of manipulating nanosized objects, which was once a dream, has become a reality and has a lot of futuristic opportunities. Moreover, the concept of carbon-based quantum dots has increased their futuristic utilities in the biomedical domain. However, the chemical approaches are considered unsafe and toxic as it utilizes various toxic chemicals that can be hazardous for living tissues; therefore, the green-based approaches are currently trending as they are safe, biocompatible, and cheap. The development of green-based carbon dots for therapeutic purposes holds an opportunistic and promising future. Although many studies and researches have shown the wide utilities of carbon dots in both therapeutic and diagnostic methods, however, due to certain disadvantages like the cost of production, cytotoxicity, prolonged uses limitations, discharge from the human body, etc., limits their uses. The researchers still need to focus on studying these limitations to increase its broad term utilities.

A.K. and K.R.B.S. contributed by Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Resources, Validation, Visualization, and Writing an original draft. M.D.G. and H.L. contributed by Data curation, Validation, and Writing an original draft. D.K. and J.S. contributed by Conceptualization, Validation, Project administration, Supervision, and Reviewing & editing the manuscript draft.
This review did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.K., M.D.G., H.L., and D.K. are thankful to their respective affiliated institutions. K.R.B.S. is thankful to Professor A.K. Singh for providing constant support and guidance and BHU (IoE grant project) for financial support throughout this work. Finally, J.S. expresses their gratitude for the DST-INSPIRE faculty Fellowship, BHU (IoE grant), and UGC New Delhi to provide financial support.
